name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Al recibir la alerta, revisé los registros.

sideB.text: Upon receiving the alert, I checked the logs.
On receiving the alert, I reviewed the logs.
Upon getting the alert, I checked the logs.

cards.note:
**[164]**



~When I recibied the alert, I checked the logs.~ is invalid due to a spelling mistake. The correct past tense is [received].

~By receiving the alert, I checked the logs.~ is not grammatically logical in this context. The preposition [by] indicates the method used to achieve a result, but you don't check logs *by means of* receiving an alert. You check them *immediately after* the alert, which requires [upon] or [on].

cards.sideA: Lo que quiero configurar es que esto haga eso.

sideB.text: What I want to set up is for this to do that.
What I want to configure is for this to do that.
What I am trying to set up is for it to do that.

cards.note: ~What I want to set up is this to do that~ and ~What I want to set up is to do that~ are not valid. When expressing that you want an object or system to perform an action, you must use the structure [for] + [object] + [to do]. The correct form is [for this to do that].

cards.sideA: Si, el esta a mi lado.

sideB.text: Yes, he's next to me.
Yes, he is right beside me.
Yes, he's by my side.
Yes, he's at my side.

cards.sideA: Tienes permitido traducir esto de español a inglés y viceversa.

sideB.text: You are allowed to translate this from Spanish to English and vice versa.
You are permitted to translate this from Spanish to English and vice versa.
You can translate this from Spanish to English and vice versa.

cards.note: ~You have allow to translate this from Spanish to English and vice versa~ is not valid because 'allow' is a verb, not a noun. You must use [You are allowed to] or [You have permission to].

sideB.text: That code was outdated.
That code was deprecated.
That code was out of date.

cards.note: ~That code was outdateded~ is not valid because the word [outdated] is already an adjective. It does not take an extra '-ed' ending.

cards.sideA: En la mañana actualice el codigo.

sideB.text: I updated the code this morning.
I pushed the code update this morning.
I refreshed the code in the morning.
I updated the code in the morning.

cards.sideA: Perdona, se me pego (congelo) el programa.

sideB.text: Sorry, my program froze.
My apologies, the program crashed on me.
Sorry, the program got stuck.

cards.note: ~Sorry, the program got froze~ and ~Sorry, the program got freeze~ are not valid because the past participle of 'freeze' is [frozen]. You can say it [got frozen], but it is much more natural to just say it [froze].

sideB.text: Thank you for being patient with me.
Thanks for your patience.
I appreciate your patience with me.
Thanks for being patient with me.

cards.sideA: Él se sentó a mi lado en la reunión.

sideB.text: He sat next to me in the meeting.
He sat beside me in the meeting.
He sat right next to me at the meeting.
He sat by me in the meeting.
He sat by my side in the meeting.

cards.note:
**[217]**

[Next to] and [beside] indicate being directly adjacent. ~Beside to me~ is incorrect.

cards.sideA: Quiero un escritorio junto a la ventana.

sideB.text: I want a desk by the window.
I'd like a desk by the window.
I want a desk right by the window.
I want a desk next to the window.
I want a desk near the window.

cards.note:
**[217]**

Using [by] is very natural to describe being right next to a window, a door, or a body of water.
